rows.Name: Xianzhe Yang
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Human-Environment Studies
Department of Kyushu-Pusan International Joint Department of Architecture and Built Environment
rows.Title of the Research: Research on the digital twin of building thermal environment utilizing digital transformation technology
rows.Outline of Research:
Achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 requires significant efforts in urban and architectural sectors, particularly in accelerating energy efficiency in residential and commercial buildings. This necessitates the development of advanced technologies and highly effective measures. To support this goal, there is a demand for high-precision and versatile numerical analysis tools compatible with cutting-edge technologies. This study aims to develop general-purpose software that realizes a digital twin of thermal environments using DX (Digital Transformation) technology, focusing on two main objectives:
 Development of an engine for building energy simulation (BES) that supports advanced technologies, including the development of a detailed mathematical model based on building physics and the construction of a coupled analysis method with Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) (Figure 1).
 Integration of the BES tool with architectural design tools.
 Figure 1. An example of the coupling method between ES and CFD

rows.Name: Tomomi SHODA
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Economics
Department of Economic System
rows.Title of the Research: CO 2 Reduction Potential of Global Supply Chain Networks: An MRIO Approach Incorporating Maritime Network Structures
rows.Outline of Research: My research focuses on CO 2 reduction potential of global supply chain (GSC) restructuring with consideration for the environmental efficiency of international shipping. 23% of global CO 2 emissions are embodied in traded goods through GSC. The restructuring of GSC can significantly reduce the carbon footprint of production. However, GSC is highly dependent on international shipping. Multi-Regional Input–Output (MRIO) tables, one of the most effective tools currently for analyzing the GSC, do not provide insights into transportation systems used for trade flows. My research aims to propose a new MRIO framework that incorporate maritime network to estimate the CO 2 reduction potential.

rows.Name: Kodai Matsumoto
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Engineering
Department of Applied Chemistry
rows.Title of the Research: Development of Three-Dimensional Chiral Plasmonic Meta-clusters with Circular Polarization Characteristics for Effective Utilization of Sunlight
rows.Outline of Research: Circular polarization has potential applications in improving the efficiency of solar cells and promoting plant growth, and the development of high-performance dichroic circular polarizers (DCP) and meta-surfaces to enhance circular polarized photoluminescence (CPL) of chiral chromophores is expected. Asymmetric plasmonic nanostructures have been reported to exhibit circular dichroism and enhance CPL due to their unique electromagnetic properties, but their effects remain limited. In this study, 3D helical chiral plasmonic meta-clusters are developed by rolling up 2D plasmonic arrays like origami to demonstrate DCP and enhancement of CPL.

rows.Name: Ryudai Ueno
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Bioresource and Environmental Sciences Graduate School
Department of Forest Environmental Science
rows.Title of the Research: Prediction of Future Afforestation Sites and Sustainable Forest Management in Kyushu
rows.Outline of Research: Forests serve as a crucial carbon sink. In order to implement afforestation aimed at increasing carbon sequestration and to achieve sustainable forest management in the future, it is essential to consider the impacts of climate change and population decline on forest management. This study predicts suitable areas for afforestation in the latter half of the 21st century, taking into account factors such as changes in temperature, precipitation, and population dynamics.

rows.Name: SHEN Siyu
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Economics
Department of Economic Systems
rows.Title of the Research: Environmental-economic trade-offs of membrane-based direct air capture technologies using life cycle assessment
rows.Outline of Research: Climate change is primarily driven by CO 2 emissions. Achieving the Paris Agreement’s target of limiting global warming to 1.5°C requires not only reducing current emissions but also removing existing CO 2 from the atmosphere. This study explores the potential of Direct Air Capture (DAC) combined with utilization (DAC-U) at a household scale. Using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), we evaluate the environmental and economic feasibility of a small-scale DAC-U system that converts CO 2 into household fuel and generates carbon credits. The analysis considers energy consumption, fuel production, carbon reduction, and carbon credit trading. If environmentally and economically viable, decentralized DAC-U units could provide a socially acceptable means for households to contribute to carbon neutrality.

rows.Name: Itsuki Oyama
rows.Affiliation:
Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Engineering Sciences
Department of Engineering Sciences
rows.Title of the Research: Optimizing heat sink in nuclear fusion reactor using Topology optimization
rows.Outline of Research: Plasma facing components (PFCs) are faced with extreme heat load inside a fusion reactor. Some useful concepts for optimizing heat deposition of PFCs are necessary. In a fast-breeder reactor, reduced-activation ferritic steel (RAFS) will be used around cooling systems inside PFCs. Then it is effective to design a heat sink with consideration about the heat conductivity of RAFS. Topology optimization can play a role to design its heat sink. The goal of this study is to propose a design for an effective heat sink inside reactor using this technique.

rows.Name: Yuki Noguchi
rows.Affiliation:
Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Engineering Sciences
Plasma and Quantum Science and Engineering Major
rows.Title of the Research: Study of F82H Precipitates Amorphization in DEMO Reactor
rows.Outline of Research: Fusion reactors are expected to be the next-generation power sources. In the conceptual design of the domestic DEMO reactor, the Reduced Activation Ferritic/Martensitic Steel (F82H) is the primary candidate material for the blanket first wall. One of the precipitates present in F82H is the Cr 23-x WxC 6 system, which has been confirmed to be amorphized under Fe 3+ ion irradiation at temperatures relevant to fusion reactor operation, raising potential concerns regarding material degradation. This study aims to elucidate the structural changes leading to the amorphization process using molecular simulation tools such as first-principles calculations and molecular dynamics.

rows.Name: Haomin FU
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Integrated Frontier Sciences
Department of Automotive Science
rows.Title of the Research: The Changing Landscape of EV Business Ecosystem: A Downstream Value Chain Analysis
rows.Outline of Research: This research explores the evolving EV business ecosystem, with a focus on downstream value chain activities such as charging infrastructure, after-sales services, and mobility solutions. Unlike traditional vehicles, EVs require new digital and service-oriented capabilities that enable continuous value creation beyond the point of sale. Through a multi-case study approach, the study examines how legacy automakers, start-ups, and cross-industry entrants are reconfiguring their resources and strategies to meet changing consumer needs. It analyzes how these actors build ecosystem partnerships, manage customer engagement, and innovate in areas like battery services and platform-based mobility. By investigating these dynamics, the research provides both theoretical insights into capability transformation under technological disruption and practical guidance for building more competitive, sustainable business models in the EV era. Ultimately, it aims to illuminate how digital and service innovations are reshaping the final stage of automotive value delivery to support a user-centric and low-carbon mobility future.

rows.Name: ZHANG KAILI
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School human-environment studies
Department of Urban Design, Planning and Disaster Management
rows.Title of the Research: The impact mechanism of multi-scale urban built environment on health and well-being of residents
rows.Outline of Research: From an interdisciplinary perspective, this study utilizes multivariate data such as remote sensing imagery, street view images, vector data, and house prices to identify the impact mechanisms of the built environment on public health outcomes (physical, mental, and social health) , based on the machine-learning statistical models including Geographically Weighted Regression Model, Random Forest Model, and Latent Growth Curve Modeling, respectively, to identify the mechanisms by which the built environment affects public health outcomes (physical, mental, and social health) at three different scales: city, block, and community. Based on the different scales of research, it is possible to obtain more comprehensive findings, which can provide quantitative guidance and insights for health-promoting urban planning and design.

rows.Name: Yuki Tomita
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Science
Department of Chemistry
rows.Title of the Research: Highly Efficient Ammonia Oxidation by a Macrocyclic Molecular Catalyst
rows.Outline of Research: These days, ammonia has attracted much attention as a new energy carrier. However, ammonia oxidation to nitrogen has not been developed well because of 6 electrons oxidation reaction. On the other hand, macrocyclic metal complexes like porphyrins or phthalocyanines have been reported as water oxidation catalysts which is 4 electrons oxidation reaction. These molecular catalysts promote water oxidation via bimolecular mechanism. The bimolecular mechanism is important to promote ammonia oxidation efficiently because 3 electrons oxidation of catalyst is enough. In this study, highly efficient ammonia oxidation is achieved by using a novel macrocyclic molecular catalyst.

rows.Name: Shogo Nakamura
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Engineering
Department of Hydrogen Energy Systems
rows.Title of the Research: Design and Evaluation of PtW-Based Catalysts for Enhancing Fuel Starvation Tolerance
rows.Outline of Research:
Polymer electrolyte fuel cells (PEFCs) have attracted attention as a core technology for utilizing renewable energy and achieving a zero-emission society.
This study focuses on fuel starvation at the anode, one of the major degradation factors in PEFCs. When hydrogen supply is temporarily interrupted, the anode potential rises sharply, leading to irreversible degradation such as carbon support corrosion and platinum catalyst dissolution. These phenomena significantly reduce cell performance and hinder long-term operation.

rows.Name: Yutong CHEN
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Human-Environment Studies
Department of Architecture
rows.Title of the Research: Development of energy-saving performance and indoor health environment diagnosis system based on BI-Tech
rows.Outline of Research: Japanese government declared goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2050, to achieve this target the Ministry of Energy has proposed the BI-Tech project, which aims to help raise awareness of energy conservation among building occupants through AI and IoT technologies. In this study, we focus on the users’ air conditioning set-point temperature and energy-saving behaviors and developed a low-cost system for real-time monitoring & prediction of indoor thermal comfort and air conditioning energy consumption based on BI-Tech, calculating thermal comfort in real time and predicting air conditioning energy consumption based on user’s set-point temperature changes and evaluate the indoor healthy office environment in real time uploading data to App interacting with users, to raise the users’ awareness of energy saving and achieve energy-saving behavior.

rows.Name: Seiya Imada
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Economics
Department of Economic Systems
rows.Title of the Research: Carbon Footprint Analysis of Japanese Wooden Houses
rows.Outline of Research: Global construction activities and associated electricity consumption account for 19% of total CO 2 emissions. Most CO 2 emission reduction measures in the Japanese residential sector are not focused on reducing CO 2 emissions from the supply chain. To reduce CO 2 emissions through the supply chain, it is necessary to identify CO 2 emission hotspots in the supply chain structure. Therefore, this study estimates the supply chain structure of wooden houses, which account for 90% of detached houses in Japan, and identifies CO 2 emission hotspots in the supply chain structure.

rows.Name: Yusuke Oga
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Economics
Department of Economic Systems
rows.Title of the Research: Carbon footprint analysis for tourism in Japan using environmentally extended input-output approach.
rows.Outline of Research: Tourism demand in Japan is expected to increase over the long term, and CO 2 emissions from tourism are also expected to increase. This study aims to develop a new input-output framework which can estimate the carbon footprint per inbound traveler by nationality and per domestic traveler by destination. The results can be used to identify travelers and industrial sectors with large CO 2 burdens, and to make demand-side policy recommendations (e.g., a carbon tax on entry into Japan and a carbon labeling system) and supply-side policy recommendations (e.g., carbon subsidies for industries with large CO 2 burdens).

rows.Name: Yuta Takaoka
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Engineering
Department of Applied Chemistry
rows.Title of the Research: Electrochemical CO 2 Reduction Catalyst for Multi-carbon Products
rows.Outline of Research: The large amount of CO 2 emission should be decreased to realize carbon neutral society. One of the technologies to reduce CO 2 emission is CO 2 reduction reaction (CO 2 RR). CO 2 RR is highly attracted because the reaction occurred under mild conditions such as room temperature and atmospheric pressure. However, CO 2 RR needs an efficient electrocatalyst to increase product selectivity and decrease overpotential. In this research, we develop the electrocatalyst by rationally designing a structure and compositing various elements to increase the catalytic activity for CO 2 RR.

rows.Name: Xuesong WEI
rows.Affiliation:
Faculty of Engineering
Department of Mechanical Engineering
rows.Title of the Research: Dramatic improvement of critical current density based on the analogy of boiling and water electrolysis
rows.Outline of Research: Renewable energy power generation has received a great deal of attention in recent years. For the demand of using those excess electricity power generated by renewable energy more efficient without wasting, there is a need to develop the alkaline water electrolysis which can transform excess generated electricity into hydrogen, as a large-scale and low-cost electricity storage technology. In the previous research, based on the analogy between boiling and water electrolysis, we applied the cooling method by using honeycomb porous plate, which had succeeded in significantly improving boiling Critical Heat Flux (CHF) to alkaline water electrolysis, and succeeded in improving the Critical Current Density (CCD) of water electrolysis. In order to clarify the mechanism of water electrolysis improvement from microscale, we developed a honeycomb porous electrode creation technology with which honeycomb porous structure can be formed by self-organization process, then plan to build a theoretical model based on the experimental results.

rows.Name: Kotaro Shinozaki
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Engineering
Department of Earth Resources Engineering
rows.Title of the Research: An Analysis and System Proposal Regarding Social Acceptance of Geothermal Development with Mathematical-Sociology Approach
rows.Outline of Research: Although Japan has the third-largest geothermal potential in the world, its installed capacity of geothermal power plants ranks in 10th globally. One of the reasons why the geothermal development has been stagnant in Japan is the oppositions from local hot spa owners. This problem is academically classified as social acceptance of geothermal development, and previous studies conducted detailed case studies to find out how to improve the social acceptance and to build a successful consensus among stakeholders. This study aims to suggest effective knowledge and systems contributing to the improvement of social acceptance of geothermal development by applying mathematical-sociology methods such as game theory and agent-based simulation to the analysis of the consensus-building situations.

rows.Name: Hiroki Isogawa
rows.Affiliation:
Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Engineering Sciences
Department of Interdisciplinary Engineering Sciences
rows.Title of the Research: Analysis of Tritium permeation behavior of Li rod simulant for development of Tritium confinement technology
rows.Outline of Research: Tritium is used as a fuel in fusion reactors, which are expected to be a next-generation energy source. Since the amount of lithium is extremely small when naturally occurring, tritium production using high-temperature gas reactors is being considered. However, tritium retention behavior in materials in high-temperature environments is still unknown, and clarification of these issues is essential from the viewpoint of safety. In this study, tritium permeation experiments will be conducted using a test specimen that simulates the core of a high-temperature gas-cooled reactor. The experimental results will be analyzed from a chemical engineering viewpoint to model the tritium permeation behavior and to elucidate the basic tritium migration phenomena.

rows.Name: Taisei Tomaru
rows.Affiliation:
Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Engineering Sciences
Department of Interdisciplinary Engineering Sciences
rows.Title of the Research: Research on the production of high-performance carbon fiber using stranded resources
rows.Outline of Research: “Stranded resources” refer to resources that have lost value due to environmental and social changes. An example is ethylene bottom oil (EO), a heavy residue oil discharged during ethylene production. This is currently used as boiler fuel and is responsible for a large amount of CO 2 emissions. Therefore, this study focuses on EO as one of the stranded resources and attempts to make effective use of EO.　The production of EO-derived high-performance carbon fiber will contribute to the removal of potential CO 2 emission sources and the construction of an innovative decarbonized society. We will propose a new and unprecedented production method to manufacture high-performance carbon fibers, aiming for low-cost and high-yield production.

rows.Affiliation: Graduate School of Engineering, Department of Mechanical Engineering
rows.Name: Tianhui Fan
rows.Title of the Research: Elucidating the mechanisms of reduce creep life in hydrogen to ensure the safety of advanced high-temperature hydrogen technologies
rows.Outline of Research: Advanced high-temperature hydrogen technologies, such as SOEC for hydrogen generation and SOFC for hydrogen power generation, are expected to play a significant role in achieving carbon neutrality by 2050. The structural components of these devices are subjected to a very aggressive environment, which is high-temperature and hydrogen. Furthermore, considering creep deformation is essential in designing mechanical components that operate at high temperature. However, the understanding of degradation of structural materials in high-temperature hydrogen remains insufficient. The objective of this study is to elucidate the mechanisms by which hydrogen affects creep deformation. To achieve this, I will collaborate internationally and interdisciplinarily with Professor Kirchheim at the University of Gottingen, who has developed the defactant theory based on thermodynamics for hydrogen embrittlement at room temperature, along with his colleagues.

rows.Affiliation: Graduate School of Integrated Frontier Sciences, Department of Automotive Science
rows.Title of the Research: Nanoparticle modified oxide as model air electrode for oxygen reduction site
rows.Outline of Research: Global warming and climate change induce serious problems in the earth’s life due to the usage of fossil fuels. These life-threatening problems must be solved by replacing fossil fuels to hydrogen, which has an important role to develop a clean, efficient, and environmental-friendly energy society. For green hydrogen production, high-temperature steam electrolysis (HTSE) is expecting to be used through Solid Oxide Electrolysis Cells (SOECs), which is reverse reaction of Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFCs), electrochemical energy-conversion devices that convert chemical energy directly to electrical energy with high efficiency and low pollution. This hybrid energy system requires highly active electrode for ORR/OER to produce hydrogen and electricity efficiently. Therefore, I study to understand active sites for ORR and OER design oxygen dissociation and association reaction. By doing so, it can decrease IR loss and overpotential to boost cell performance as well as improve the reversibility and durability of the single cell.

rows.Affiliation: Graduate School of Engineering, Department of Hydrogen Energy Systems
rows.Title of the Research: Exploration of rate-limiting factors and development of design guidelines for MH alloy systems
rows.Outline of Research: To achieve the goal of a hydrogen society, a safer, more mass- and volume-efficient, and less expensive method of hydrogen storage is essential. The metal hydride alloys (MH alloys) are a safer alternative to high-pressure tanks, and the first step in using MH alloy systems is to increase the filling and releasing rates from MH alloy tanks. To this end, we will comprehensively examine the processes from the surface of MH alloy particles to the internal diffusion and phase change processes and clarify the rate-limiting factors of the MH alloy system. Then, we optimize the filling and releasing time to minimize the representative times for solid-phase diffusion, gas-phase diffusion, and heat transfer.

rows.Name: Sora Matsushima
rows.Title of the Research: Development and Empirical Analysis of a Comprehensive LCA Assessment Framework Considering Housing Stock Dynamics.
rows.Outline of Research: CO 2 emissions from the housing and building sectors in Japan are said to account for about 40% of the total. While various measures have been taken to reduce CO 2 emissions, a detailed analysis of the dichotomous relationship between the reduction of CO 2 emissions during construction and the increase during use by extending the service life of houses, and the increase of CO 2 emissions during construction and the reduction during use by the spread of energy-saving houses has not yet been conducted. This study estimates the life-cycle CO 2 emissions of Japanese housing as a whole, taking into account the life span of the building as a whole and the composition of the stock by age, and investigates the trade-off relationship that occurs with the proliferation of longer-lived and energy-efficient housing through scenario analysis.

rows.Name: Daisuke Yoshizawa
rows.Affiliation: Graduate School of Economics Department of Economic Systems
rows.Title of the Research: CO 2 Reduction Potential of Expanding Car Sharing Services
rows.Outline of Research: While it is important to change the way we use cars in order to decarbonize, there is little research on the environmental impact of car sharing services, which significantly change the way we use cars. We analyze how CO 2 emissions in Japan will change when car sharing services become widespread, and make recommendations that will lead to global warming mitigation policies. This study applies a stock-flow model of passenger cars to develop a framework that can comprehensively analyze changes in CO 2 emissions when car-sharing services expand, and quantitatively analyze the role of the car-sharing service diffusion in reducing CO 2 emissions.

rows.Name: Shinichi Takeno
rows.Title of the Research: Development of new electronic-Li-ionic conductors and their battery application
rows.Outline of Research: To achieve a carbon-free society, the development of excellent secondary batteries is one of the priority issues. Li-ion batteries and Li-Air batteries are attracting attention due to their high energy densities. Moreover, an all-solid-state battery based on Li conductive oxide is expected as the next-generation battery with high energy density and high safety. Here, in this study, I aim to develop new materials that show high electronic-Li-ionic conductivity. I am trying to establish the material design for excellent mixed conductors by a design of experiments and apply those materials for battery applications.

rows.Name: Yixin Chen
rows.Title of the Research: Construction of high-capacity all-solid-state battery using hydride negative electrode
rows.Outline of Research: So far, it has been clarified that when we use the high-capacity negative electrode materials such as Mg(BH 4 ) 2 , Ca(BH 4 ) 2 , and MgH 2 , solid electrolytes can be generated by electrode reaction. As the electrode reaction products of MgH 2 , LiH and Mg will form by Li insertion, and despite the low Li ion conductivity of LiH, it can be used as an excellent negative electrode of all-solid-state battery due to the self-generation of solid electrolyte. In this study, it is necessary to clarify the ionic conductivity of the formed Li salt and the three-dimensional distribution of reactants, products, and carbon in the electrode mixture during charging and discharging for the further understanding of the factor than affect the battery characteristic of the negative electrode that using the self-generated solid electrolyte.

rows.Name: Zifei Nie
rows.Research topic: Toward Safer, Smarter, and Decarbonized Mobility: Personalized Energy-efficient Driving for Intelligent Vehicles
rows.Reseacrh outline: Analyzing the energy consumption for road vehicles and the corresponding driving behaviors are critical tasks for the realization of public traffic with a low energy cost and high efficiency. Especially, intelligent and connected vehicular technologies potentiate the energy-saving driving with ubiquitous and sensible traffic information. In this research, we try to utilize the advanced optimal control algorithm to optimize the driving decision and control output from vehicle powertrain so that the relationship between vehicle driving, road condition, traffic situation and powertrain efficiency can be fully matched to realize the energy-efficient driving. Meanwhile, the personalized driving styles are expected to be incorporated into the automated driving control system to quantitatively evaluate the impact from driving style on energy consumption. Ultimately, a human-oriented, safer, smarter and decarbonized energy-efficient autonomous driving system is expected to be developed for the next-generation mobility.

rows.Name: Tatsuya Hamashima
rows.Title of the Research: Development of a high-efficient DRM system and elucidation of the reaction mechanisms
rows.Outline of Research: Dry reforming of methane (DRM) has been widely studied as a GHG reduction technology. However, this process requires high temperature and induces severe coking, which causes catalytic deactivation. A previous study revealed that microwave-assisted La-Ce-Ni catalyst has high catalytic activity but elucidating specific reaction mechanisms is still needed for further energy reduction and catalyst durability. This research focuses on developing a microwave-assisted DRM system through Raman spectroscopy, XAFS analysis, and electron microscopy on La-Ce-Ni catalysts.

rows.Name: Park Hyun-Gyu
rows.Title of the Research: Development of Advanced Computer Simulated Person for Indoor Thermal Comfort and Air Quality Assessment
rows.Outline of Research:
Many comprehensive evaluation methods for IEQ (Indoor Environmental Quality) using numerical analysis such as Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) have been proposed.
Also, the reproduction of human in indoor space is regarded as a key point to evaluate IEQ with high accuracy.
Against this background, the Computer-Simulated Person (CSP), which can treat specific characteristics of the human body, has been suggested as a “digital twin” for CFD simulation. However, previous research simplified or ignored the clothing on CSP and heat and mass transfer around cloth.
So, my research aims to develop a new CSP with clothing and advanced estimation method of IEQ. This research focused on the 3 major tasks: 1) To establish an advanced thermal comfort analysis method with clothing 2) Integration of developed CSP with the human airway model 3) To suggest energy-efficient HVAC control method using the developed CSP.

rows.Name: HE QINGYI
rows.Title of the Research: 人間の省エネ意識と省エネ行動の関係分析に基づく建物エネルギーシミュレーションツールの開発
rows.Outline of Research: 本研究の目的は、将来の建物のエネルギーシミュレーションにおいて、人間の意識や行動の変化が及ぼす影響をシミュレートするためのツールを開発することである。本研究ではまず、アンケートと検証実験により、人間の環境心理と行動の関係の数理モデルを作る。そして、異なる人々に、環境意識を高めて省エネ行動を誘発するための異なる手段を設定する。これをもとに人間の行動をモデル化し、エネルギー消費のシミュレーションと組み合わせる。最後に、人間の意識や行動がエネルギー消費に与える影響を分析するシミュレーションツールを開発する。

rows.Name: Phua Yin Kan
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Engineering
Department of Applied Chemistry
rows.Title of the Research: Development of a Chemically Explainable Machine Learning Model that Allows Membrane Materials Exploration for Anion Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell
rows.Outline of Research: Beginning from functional polymeric materials such as anion exchange membranes (AEM) used in AEM fuel cells (AEMFC), this study plans on representing complete monomer structure of functional polymers in the form of graphs, and use graph neural networks with attention mechanism to improve prediction interpretability and accuracy of prediction model. Through this study, a prediction model that can feedback useful polymer structure design guidelines to experimentalist will be built, thereby realizing a model that is highly interpretable, generalizable and carries the ability to act as a materials design guideline for experimentalists. This study also helps to accelerate the realization of decarbonized society through contributing to the research and development of AEMFC, a type of FC that is essential to achieve the decarbonization goal.

rows.Name: Ryoma Sato
rows.Affiliation: Graduate School of Bioresource and Bioenvironmental Sciences, Department of Bioresource Sciences
rows.Title of the Research: Realization of decarbonization coexisting with food production: Creation of environmentally resilient biomass energy resource crops through photosynthetic modification based on functional genomics.
rows.Outline of Research: This study aims to utilize stress-tolerant plants as alternative biofuel crops grown in non-crop areas to secure stable biomass energy resource crops and create new resource crops resilient to environmental stress. We will use bioinformatics to comprehensively analyze the structure and function of genes and non-genetic regions related to high-stress tolerance and photosynthetic conversion mechanisms and to modify the genes and non-translational regions in the target plants. In addition, we will establish a cultivation method to enhance the components of biofuels and other raw materials and establish a production method for biofuel crops that does not compete with food crops.

rows.Name: Jacqueline Andrea Hidalgo Jiménez
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School Integrated Frontier Science
Department of Automotive Science
rows.Title of the Research: High-entropy ceramics as new family of photocatalysts for hydrogen production and CO 2 conversion: Ab initio calculations and experiments
rows.Outline of Research:
The utilization of fossil fuels has caused global warming, and transportation is one of the most contaminated industries. Hydrogen utilization and CO 2 conversion to functional products are two alternatives to reduce emissions. Both processes can be carried out through photocatalysis which uses light in the presence of a semiconductor to achieve a chemical reaction. Among several materials, high entropy ceramics demonstrated good properties as photocatalysts.
Objective:
A. Design new high entropy ceramics with outstanding photocatalytic activity.
B. Clarify the mechanism of photocatalytic activity and band structure of materials
C. Compare DFT calculations with experimental results

rows.Name: Muhammad Irfan Maulana Kusdhany
rows.Affiliation:
Graduate School of Integrated Frontier Sciences
Department of Automotive Science
rows.Title of the Research: Data-Driven Design of Carbon Materials for Hydrogen Storage
rows.Outline of Research:
In the transport sector, decarbonization is mainly done by converting combustion vehicles to battery electric vehicles. However, the low gravimetric energy capacity and long recharging times of batteries necessitate another solution for heavy duty transport. Hydrogen can fulfill this role due to its high energy density and quick refueling capability. One remaining issue is its storage: hydrogen has a low volumetric density. Thus, it needs to be either compressed to 70 MPa or liquefied to be stored on-board. Unfortunately, these solutions still suffer from low efficiency and high costs.
 I study an alternative method of hydrogen storage: densifying hydrogen by adsorbing it onto high surface area carbons. This method is cheaper than 70 MPa compression, and it loses less energy than liquefaction. To further improve the materials used, I use data science techniques to clearly quantify the processing-structure-property relationships affecting the hydrogen uptake performance of high surface area carbon materials.

rows.Name: Yulu Chen
rows.Title of the Research: Performance study of a passive dehumidification system naturally controls the indoor hygrothermal environment using renewable energy.
rows.Outline of Research: To improve the energy-saving performance of passive dehumidification and solar collection (PDSC) envelope system, which naturally cools and dehumidifies indoor air in summer and collects solar heat in winter, we develop a novel PSE (PDSC system combined with energy recovery ventilation) to reduce heat loads of air-conditioning system. Based on the results of numerical experiments of PSE conducted so far, we will jointly develop PSE equipment with private companies to commercialize it for general use. In addition, we will conduct outdoor experiments in a demonstration house and numerical simulations to verify efficient operating conditions and methods for PSE throughout the year and design an optimal control system.

rows.Affiliation: Graduate School of Economics, Department of Economic Systems
rows.Name: Tianhui Fan
rows.Title of the Research: The impact of compact cities on carbon emissions
rows.Outline of Research: Cities play an important role in greenhouse gas emissions. In recent years, ideas about sustainable urban development have evolved from the point of view of low-carbon cities, in which the concept of a ‘compact city’ has been proposed. After conducting a thorough literature review, I identified that previous studies have mainly concentrated on the definition, formation, characteristics, quantification, and policy evaluation of compact cities. But there is still a gap in the academic knowledge about how compact cities have impact on urban sustainability, especially on carbon emission reduction. The study will attach importance to policy-driven compact cities, combining literature review, case study and empirical research to examine the relationship and to clarify the impact mechanisms between various aspects of compact cities and carbon emissions.

rows.Name: Kento Komatsubara
rows.Title of the Research: Environmental sustainability assessment using high-resolution spatial information
rows.Outline of Research: The purpose of this study is to develop a quantitative index that can be used as a material for considering the trade-off between the introduction of renewable energy and the preservation of ecosystems on high (250m x 250m) resolution. Based on spatial information and information on revealed and stated preferences of households and industrial sectors, we will evaluate the determinants of preferences for renewable energy and ecosystems using contingent valuation method, conjoint analysis, and multi-criteria decision analysis. Based on this, we will predict the dynamics of renewable energy and ecosystems, and construct and evaluate an environmental sustainability assessment system.

rows.Name: Toraharu Watanabe
rows.Title of the Research: The research on ventilation control of ship engine room considering ammonia fuel
rows.Outline of Research: Greenhouse gas emissions from ships, especially international shipping, have been a major factor in global warming and IMO set a goal to reduce GHG emissions from international shipping. Ammonia fuel is one of the next-generation marine fuels that realizes decarbonization. On the other hand, ammonia has strong toxicity. Hence strict measures are required in terms of risk management, and regulations regarding ammonia as a fuel are being developed. In this study, to present an evaluation index for rule development, we analyse the risk of ammonia leakage in the ship engine room and optimize the ventilation control in the case of ammonia leakage by numerical fluid calculation and model experiments.

rows.Name: Timothee Redarce
rows.Title of the Research: Breakthrough of 900MPa barrier of fatigue crack growth resistance in BCC steels in high-pressure hydrogen environment
rows.Outline of Research:
Hydrogen is promised an important future with a key role for a carbon-free society. However, it is often responsible for the degradation of strength properties of the material storing it, leading to limited storing capacity and safety issues.
The goal of the research is to increase the resistance of low-alloy steels to fatigue crack growth rate in hydrogen environment. Doing so will allow the design of safer and higher-capacity hydrogen storage containers. Improving the materials’ strengths will be done through microstructure refinement (ausforming process). This research will be the first to study fatigue crack growth properties in hydrogen gas on ausformed low-alloy steels.

rows.Name: Daiki Nishimura
rows.Title of the Research: Experimental research of non-locality of turbulence in magnetized plasma.
rows.Outline of Research: Understanding the physical mechanism of turbulent transport in magnetized plasmas and suppressing it is essential for the　realization of fusion reactors, which are expected to be the next generation large-scale power plants. In magnetized plasmas, microscopic turbulence coexists and interacts with macroscopic structures and flows, resulting in the development of non-locality. PLATO at the Research Institute of Applied Mechanics, Kyushu University is the first tokamak specific to turbulence measurements and has a lot of measurement ports. In this research, the role of non-locality in turbulent transport will be studied by using tomography measurement of the global structure and a newly developed composite probe array.

rows.Name: Keitaro Maeno
rows.Title of the Research: CO2 mitigation through global supply chain restructuring
rows.Outline of Research: Automotive industries have large CO 2 emissions from their global supply chain. In order to realize a decarbonized society, there is urgent need for automotive industries to build a green supply chain, taking the opportunity of the restructuring of GSC in major countries caused by COVID-19. To build a green supply chain, it is important for an industry to identify the CO 2 emission hotspots in the its GSC and to reduce CO 2 emissions from these hotspots intensively. Therefore, this study develops a new frame work that can estimate the impact of the restructuring of Japan’s automotive industry which eliminates the CO 2 emission hotspots on the global carbon footprint, and proposes appropriate climate policies for the restructuring to reduce comprehensive CO 2 emissions from the newly reformed GSC of Japan’s automotive industry.

rows.Name: Haruka Mitoma
rows.Title of the Research: A carbon footprint analysis considering the production activities of the informal sector
rows.Outline of Research: The informal sector is a set of activities that are imperfectly regulated by the governments, and it is hardly accounted on official statistics. In emerging and developing countries, such unofficial economic activities generate one-third of the total GDP, hence the informal sector is the vital for supply chains in the regions. On the other hand, many study have demonstrated that the higher informality of economies have negative impacts on environment. This study is the first attempt to construct an extended input-output table that distinguishes the formal sector and the informal sector to explore how the informal sector contributes carbon footprint of the country.

rows.Name: Sun Mingxu
rows.Title of the Research: Electrochemical CO 2 Conversion to Value-added Chemicals
rows.Outline of Research: Electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into C 2+- products is a highly attractive route for CO 2 utilization and decreasing global warming. However, the selectivity and yield of C 2+- products remains low. Here I focus on the catalyst development and setup design to increase selectivity and yield of C 2+- products via electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 .

rows.Name: Likhith Manjunatha
rows.Title of the Research: Addressing fuel starvation based degradation in polymer electrolyte fuel cells
rows.Outline of Research:
I am excited to be part of the movement that is bringing hydrogen based technology to society. It is inevitable that hydrogen based fuel cell vehicles will occupy a strong place in future society. For now, improving fuel cell durability and performance while also driving down costs remains to be the last technological obstacle before widespread adoption. The anode side of the fuel cell is especially prone to degradation due to fuel starvation, which occurs under extreme operating conditions.
I am currently working on reducing the anode degradation modes at high humidity operating conditions by developing novel catalyst and gas diffusion layers. Ideally, by using a reversal tolerant, non-platinum based catalyst and a surface modified Titanium mesh based gas diffusion layer, I hope to achieve low cost, high performance and durable materials for automobile fuel cell applications.

rows.Name: Xiaofeng Shen
rows.Title of the Research: Highly activated single-layer nanosheet dye-sensitized photocatalyst for overall water splitting
rows.Outline of Research:
Overall water splitting using a photocatalyst and solar energy has attracted significant attention as a next-generation energy medium, but the current light-hydrogen conversion efficiency is about 1%.
Therefore, I will focus on nanosheet structures which can be improved the surface area, photon absorption efficiency, and dye loading amount.
In this research, I will develop an exfoliation reagent that has a dye-sensitizing ability, and a hybrid photocatalyst with a nanosheet type photocatalyst system that can respond to near infrared region.
